title: Communicating AI Value
description: Learn to communicate the value, capabilities, and limitations of AI features to diverse stakeholders — from engineering teams to executives to customers.
learningObjectives: Tailor AI communication to different audiences — engineers, executives, and customers — using frameworks appropriate for each group's priorities
Frame AI capabilities honestly and compellingly without overpromising or creating unrealistic expectations
Handle skepticism, pushback, and misconceptions about AI with confidence and evidence-based responses

sections.title: Understanding Your Audience
sections.content:
Every stakeholder group thinks about AI differently. Engineers want to know how it works. Executives want to know what it's worth. Customers want to know what it does for them. Communicating AI value effectively means translating the same underlying capability into three fundamentally different stories. And here's the practical twist: you can use Claude itself to help you draft these communications, tailoring the tone, depth, and framing for each audience.

**Talking to Engineers**

Engineers are your most technically literate audience, but that doesn't mean they want a deep dive on transformer architecture. What engineers actually care about is far more practical.

First and foremost, engineers want to understand **integration complexity**: how the feature fits into the existing stack, which APIs are involved, and what the latency budget looks like. They're evaluating scope of work, not listening for a sales pitch. Closely related is their concern about **failure modes**. What happens when Claude returns a bad response? What's the fallback? Engineers respect PMs who've thought through edge cases, not just the happy path.

Beyond the initial build, engineers think about the **iteration model**. They want to know how prompts will be updated, who owns prompt quality, and whether there's a testing framework. In other words, they care about the maintenance story, not just the launch story. And because engineers think in systems, they'll want clarity on **data flow** — what user data gets sent to Claude, what comes back, and where it's stored. Presenting a clear data architecture diagram earns you credibility before the Q&A even starts.

The biggest mistake PMs make with engineers is overselling. Saying "Claude can handle that" when you haven't tested the edge case destroys credibility. Instead, be precise: "Claude handles standard cases well. We need to test these three edge cases and build fallbacks."

A useful workflow: use Claude to draft your **engineer-facing spec** by providing it with pilot results, architecture notes, and known edge cases. Ask it to produce a document that covers integration points, failure handling, and the iteration plan. You'll still need to review the output with your engineering lead, but Claude accelerates the drafting and ensures you don't miss key sections.

**Talking to Executives**

Executives operate at a different altitude. They don't need to understand how Claude processes tokens — they need to understand why this investment matters and what success looks like.

The single most important shift to make when addressing executives is leading with **business outcomes over technology**. "We're reducing support resolution time by 35%" beats "We're implementing an LLM-powered response suggestion system" every time. Executives also think in competitive terms, so providing **competitive context** — where competitors stand with AI, whether you're keeping pace, catching up, or leading — helps them position the decision within their broader strategy.

Executives value PMs who are upfront about **risk and timeline**. What could go wrong? How long until results are visible? A realistic plan earns more trust than an optimistic one. Finally, how you frame costs matters enormously. Present AI expenses as **investments with expected returns**, not line items. "This feature costs $50K/year in API fees and saves $400K/year in support costs" is a story executives can act on.

In practice, Claude is excellent at drafting **executive briefings and stakeholder updates**. Feed it your raw pilot metrics, the business context, and the audience (VP of Product, CEO, board), and ask for a concise summary that leads with outcomes and frames costs as investments. Claude handles the tone shift from technical to strategic naturally, and you can iterate on the framing in minutes instead of hours.

**Talking to Customers**

Customers are the hardest audience because their expectations are shaped by AI hype in the media. They've seen demos that look like magic and assume your product should work the same way.

The key principle is to **focus on the outcome, not the technology**. Customers don't care that you're using Claude. They care that their reports get written faster, their data gets analyzed automatically, or their questions get answered instantly. Alongside this, you need to **set honest expectations**. Saying "This feature handles about 80% of cases automatically and gives you a starting point for the rest" is better than promising perfection and delivering frustration. Finally, **emphasizing user control** makes a real difference in adoption. Customers feel more comfortable with AI when they know they're in charge — "You can review, edit, or dismiss any AI suggestion" reduces anxiety and builds trust.

You can also use Claude to draft your **stakeholder communications** — customer announcements, changelog entries, help center articles — by prompting it with the feature details and explicitly asking for outcome-focused language with no technical jargon. Review and edit the output to match your product's voice, but let Claude handle the first draft so you can focus on the strategic framing.

playground.description: Practice using Claude to draft the same feature announcement for different audiences. See how changing the audience in your prompt shifts the tone, detail level, and emphasis.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a communication coach helping a PM draft stakeholder communications about AI features. For each request, produce a concise, well-structured draft appropriate for the specified audience. Use concrete language, avoid buzzwords, and match the formality and technical depth to the audience.
playground.userMessage:
I need to communicate about a new Claude-powered feature that auto-categorizes incoming support tickets by department with 85% accuracy. Our pilot ran for 3 weeks with the support team and reduced average routing time from 4 minutes to 30 seconds. API costs are $0.02 per ticket.

Please draft a short communication (3-5 sentences) about this feature for my VP of Engineering. Focus on what the eng team needs to know for the production build.

adaptedExamples.product-manager: When pitching a Claude-powered ticket-routing feature, you'd tell your engineering lead about the API integration points and fallback logic, while telling your VP about the 35% reduction in resolution time and $180K annual savings. Using Claude to draft audience-specific briefs lets you iterate on framing in minutes instead of hours.

sections.title: Framing AI Capabilities Without Overpromising
sections.content:
The language you use to describe AI features shapes expectations, and mismatched expectations are the primary source of AI feature disappointment. Overpromise and users feel betrayed. Underpromise and stakeholders won't fund the project. The sweet spot is confident honesty.

**The Capability Framing Framework**

For every AI feature, prepare three statements that together paint a complete and credible picture.

Start with **what the feature does well**, leading with a specific strength backed by evidence. For example: "Claude accurately categorizes 85% of support tickets into the correct department based on our testing with 2,000 historical tickets." Then address **where it has limits**, being direct about boundaries without being apologetic: "It's less reliable with tickets that span multiple departments or contain highly technical jargon specific to our enterprise clients." Finally, show **how it improves** by describing the path forward: "We're iterating on the prompts weekly based on miscategorized tickets, and accuracy has improved 12 percentage points in the last month."

This framework works with every audience. Adjust the technical depth, but keep the structure.

**Language Patterns to Use**

Precise language builds trust, while vague language invites misinterpretation. The words you choose carry implicit promises, so it pays to be deliberate.

Say "assists" instead of "automates" unless the process is truly end-to-end with no human involvement. Say "generates a draft" instead of "writes" — it signals that human review is part of the workflow. When stating performance claims, qualify them with phrases like "in our testing" or "based on current data" rather than making unconditional assertions. Qualifying your statements doesn't make them weaker — it makes them credible. Similarly, say "improves over time" instead of "learns," because "learns" implies autonomous intelligence that can create unrealistic expectations.

**Language Patterns to Avoid**

Certain phrases reliably cause problems, and they're worth learning to recognize.

Using **"AI-powered"** as the only description of a feature is essentially meaningless. Every chatbot is "AI-powered." What specifically does your feature do? The phrase **"it understands"** is similarly problematic — Claude processes text and generates responses, and saying it "understands" your users implies a level of cognition that creates unrealistic expectations. Claiming **"it never makes mistakes"** sets you up for a trust-destroying moment when the first error inevitably surfaces. And describing the experience as **"just like talking to a person"** misleads users into expecting human-level nuance, leading to disappointment when edge cases arise.

**The Confidence Spectrum**

Matching your confidence level to your evidence is one of the most important habits you can build as a PM communicating about AI.

When you have robust testing data, use **high confidence** language: "Based on testing with 5,000 real examples, this feature correctly extracts invoice data 92% of the time." For features with limited but positive data, shift to **medium confidence**: "In early testing, this feature shows promising results for summarizing meeting transcripts, with most test users accepting the output with minor edits." And for early-stage explorations, use **low confidence** framing: "We're exploring whether Claude can assist with code review suggestions. Initial experiments look promising, but we need more testing before committing to a timeline." The goal isn't to hedge excessively — it's to ensure your audience knows exactly how much weight to put behind each claim.

adaptedExamples.product-manager: Instead of telling stakeholders your AI summarization feature 'automatically writes reports,' frame it precisely: 'Generates draft summaries that our team accepts without edits 73% of the time, based on four weeks of pilot data.' This Capability-Limit-Improvement structure lets you present an honest, fundable story.

sections.title: Creating Compelling Demos and Proof-of-Concepts
sections.content:
A great demo can secure buy-in for an AI feature faster than any slide deck. A bad demo can kill a project. The difference usually comes down to preparation, not the underlying technology.

**The Demo Paradox**

AI demos face a unique paradox: if the demo goes perfectly, stakeholders assume the feature is done and wonder why you need more time to ship. If the demo shows errors, stakeholders question whether the feature is viable. You need to thread a needle — showing genuine capability while setting honest expectations.

**Structuring an Effective AI Demo**

The best AI demos follow a specific narrative arc rather than simply showcasing features in isolation.

**Start with the pain.** Show the manual workflow first and let stakeholders feel the tedium, complexity, or time cost of the current process. A 30-second clip of an agent manually searching for the right knowledge base article makes the AI solution feel like a relief rather than a novelty.

From there, **show the happy path** by demonstrating the AI feature on a case where it works well. Use a real example, not a contrived one — stakeholders can smell a cherry-picked demo. But don't stop at the success story. The next step is to intentionally **show a realistic challenge** where the AI needs human judgment. This builds enormous credibility: "Here's a complex ticket where Claude provides a starting point, but the agent adds important nuance." Showing a limitation isn't weakness — it's realism.

Finally, **end with the improvement story.** Show how the feature gets better over time. If you have before/after data from prompt iterations, present it. "Three weeks ago, acceptance rate was 52%. After prompt refinements based on agent feedback, it's now 71%." This narrative arc — pain, solution, honest limitation, growth trajectory — is far more compelling than a flawless showcase.

**Building a Proof-of-Concept**

A PoC isn't a demo — it's a time-boxed experiment designed to answer specific questions. The distinction matters because a demo is about persuasion while a PoC is about learning. Structure your PoC to test the riskiest assumptions first.

Before writing a single line of code, **define success criteria.** What acceptance rate, accuracy, or latency would make this feature worth pursuing? Write it down before the PoC starts so that you're measuring against a standard, not rationalizing results after the fact.

Equally important is using **real data, not synthetic data.** Claude's performance on carefully crafted test cases doesn't predict its performance on messy real-world inputs. Feed it actual customer support tickets, real meeting transcripts, or genuine user queries. You should also **time-box ruthlessly** — a PoC should take days, not weeks. If you can't demonstrate viability in a few days of prompt engineering and testing, the feature may need a fundamentally different approach.

Perhaps most counterintuitively, the best PoCs **document failures, not just successes.** The most valuable output of a PoC is understanding what doesn't work. Failure patterns inform your prompt strategy, guardrail design, and scope decisions for the real feature.

**Common Demo Mistakes**

A few pitfalls come up repeatedly, and they're all avoidable with preparation. Running a **live demo without a backup** is risky because Claude's responses vary — always have pre-recorded outputs ready for high-stakes presentations. **Starting with edge cases** is another common error; lead with common, representative examples instead, since edge cases are important but shouldn't be the first thing stakeholders see. If your demo has a visible delay, **address the latency proactively** by noting that response time in production will be faster — otherwise stakeholders will fixate on the pause. And never **skip the cost conversation**. Stakeholders who see a compelling demo will immediately ask "why not roll this out to everyone tomorrow?" Have the cost-per-request numbers ready so you can answer with confidence.

adaptedExamples.product-manager: Before demoing your Claude-powered ticket classifier to the leadership team, show the manual routing workflow first so they feel the pain. Then demo a real ticket that classifies correctly, followed by an ambiguous ticket where Claude flags it for human review. End with your improvement data: accuracy up 12 points in one month.

sections.title: Handling Pushback and Skepticism
sections.content:
Pushback on AI features is healthy and inevitable. Skeptical stakeholders aren't enemies — they're stress-testing your thinking. The best PMs welcome pushback because it makes the product strategy stronger.

**Common Objections and How to Address Them**

Certain objections come up in nearly every AI initiative. Learning to address them confidently — without being dismissive — is one of the most valuable skills you can develop.

When someone says **"AI is just a fad,"** the worst thing you can do is argue that AI is transformative. Instead, acknowledge the hype honestly: "You're right that there's a lot of hype around AI. That's exactly why we're focused on measurable outcomes, not buzzwords. Our pilot showed a 35% reduction in ticket resolution time — that's not hype, that's a business result." Ground every claim in specific data from your own testing, not industry trends.

The **hallucination and errors objection** is one you should welcome, because it gives you a chance to show how seriously you've thought about failure modes. Don't minimize the concern — validate it: "Hallucination is a real risk, and we've designed for it. Human review is built into the workflow, Claude never takes autonomous actions on behalf of the user, and we track error rates weekly. Our current hallucination rate on this task is under 3%." Demonstrating that you've designed for failure, not just success, builds enormous trust.

The concern that **"this will replace people's jobs"** requires genuine sensitivity. Frame AI as augmentation, not replacement, and mean it: "This feature handles the repetitive parts of the workflow so agents can focus on complex cases that require human judgment. Our pilot agents reported higher job satisfaction because they spent less time on copy-paste tasks." That said, if headcount reduction IS part of the strategy, be honest with leadership about it rather than pretending otherwise.

When stakeholders worry they **don't have the data or infrastructure**, scope the requirements realistically: "Claude works with the data we already have. We don't need a data warehouse or ML pipeline. The first version uses prompt engineering with our existing knowledge base, and we can iterate from there." The Claude API dramatically lowers the infrastructure bar compared to traditional ML, and this is often a genuine surprise to stakeholders accustomed to the old paradigm.

Finally, the **cost objection** deserves a complete answer, not a defensive one. Present the full equation: "Each Claude API call costs approximately $0.03 for this use case. An agent handling this task manually costs $0.85 per ticket in salary time. Even at a 60% automation rate, we save $0.48 per ticket. At our volume, that's $180K annually." Always compare AI costs to the current cost of doing business, not to zero.

**The Skeptic Conversion Playbook**

Some stakeholders won't be convinced by arguments alone. They need to see it work in their specific context, on their terms.

The most effective approach is to **offer a time-boxed pilot.** Proposing something like "Let's run this for two weeks with one team and measure the results — if the data doesn't support continuing, we stop" dramatically lowers resistance because the commitment feels reversible.

Within that pilot, **let skeptics choose the test cases.** When a skeptic picks the hardest examples they can think of and Claude handles them reasonably well, the credibility boost is enormous — far more powerful than if you'd chosen favorable examples yourself. Throughout the process, **share raw data, not just summaries.** Skeptics trust data they can interrogate, so give them access to the actual outputs, user feedback, and metrics rather than a polished summary that feels like marketing.

Perhaps the most underrated tactic is to **find an internal champion.** One respected engineer or team lead who uses the AI feature successfully and speaks positively about it is worth more than any number of slide decks. Peer credibility is the most powerful force in organizational change.

playground.description: Practice responding to stakeholder pushback on an AI feature. Try different objections and see how framing your response with data and empathy changes the outcome.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a skeptical VP of Operations at a mid-size SaaS company. The PM (the user) is pitching you on a Claude-powered feature. Push back realistically — raise concerns about cost, reliability, job displacement, or hype. Respond to their arguments thoughtfully: if they provide strong data and honest framing, acknowledge it. If they hand-wave or oversell, press harder. Stay in character throughout.
playground.userMessage: I'd like to walk you through the results of our AI pilot for support ticket routing. We've been testing Claude-powered auto-categorization for the past three weeks and I think the results make a strong case for a full rollout. Can I share the data with you?

adaptedExamples.product-manager: When your VP of Operations says 'AI is just a fad,' resist arguing about industry trends. Instead, respond with your own pilot data: 'Our three-week test reduced routing time from 4 minutes to 30 seconds at $0.02 per ticket versus $0.85 in agent time.' Offering a time-boxed pilot with the skeptic choosing the test cases is the fastest path to buy-in.

sections.title: Write an AI Feature Brief for Different Audiences
sections.content: Practice tailoring your AI communication to different stakeholders by writing the same feature description three different ways.

exercise.prompt:
You're the PM for a Claude-powered feature that automatically summarizes long customer feedback threads (email chains, support conversations, survey responses) into structured insights — key themes, sentiment trends, and recommended actions. The feature has been in pilot for 4 weeks with the Customer Success team.

Pilot results: 73% of summaries accepted without edits, average time saved per feedback review reduced from 12 minutes to 3 minutes, customer success managers report higher confidence in quarterly business reviews because they're reviewing 3x more feedback.

Write three versions of a feature brief — one for each audience:

1. **Engineering Brief** (2-3 paragraphs) — What does the engineering team need to know to scope the production build? Cover integration points, data flow, failure modes, and iteration plan.
2. **Executive Summary** (1 paragraph, 4-5 sentences) — What does your VP of Product need to know to approve funding for the full rollout? Focus on business impact and investment.
3. **Customer Announcement** (1 paragraph, 3-4 sentences) — What do customers need to know? Focus on what the feature does for them and how they stay in control.

For each version, explicitly note: what information you included that's unique to that audience, and what information you deliberately left out and why.
hints: Engineers care about the 27% of summaries that needed edits — what went wrong and how will you address it? They also want to know about model selection, latency requirements, and data handling.
Your VP of Product wants to see the ROI: time saved x number of CSMs x hourly cost. They also want to know the competitive angle — are competitors offering this?
Customers don't care about Claude, acceptance rates, or API costs. They care that their feedback is being heard and acted on, and that they can review what the AI produces.
The deliberate omissions are as important as the inclusions — explain your reasoning for what you left out of each version
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) An engineering brief that covers integration points (API, data pipeline), failure handling, model selection rationale, latency requirements, and an iteration plan — vague statements like 'we'll use Claude' without specifying the model or architecture are insufficient. (2) An executive summary that quantifies business impact (time saved, coverage increase, projected cost savings at scale), mentions the investment required, and frames the competitive context — should avoid technical jargon. (3) A customer announcement that describes the feature in outcome terms (not technology terms), emphasizes user control and review capability, and sets appropriate expectations — should not mention Claude, acceptance rates, or internal metrics. (4) For each version, explicit notes on what was included uniquely for that audience and what was deliberately omitted with reasoning. Deduct points for briefs that don't differentiate meaningfully between audiences, that use jargon inappropriate for the audience, or that omit the deliberate inclusion/exclusion analysis.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content:
Before moving on, test your understanding by considering these questions. If any feel uncertain, revisit the relevant section above.

Think about the difference between what engineers and executives need to hear. What is the single most important thing engineers want to know about an AI feature that executives typically don't care about?

Consider why describing a feature as "AI-powered" — without further context — falls flat with customers. What's missing from that description, and what would be more effective?

When a skeptical stakeholder says "AI is just hype," what type of evidence is most effective at changing their mind? Think about whether industry trends or internal data carries more weight.

Finally, reflect on the demo paradox. What's the risk of giving a flawless AI demo with no errors shown, and how might that backfire even though the demo itself went perfectly?

exercise.prompt:
Your CEO has asked you to send a one-paragraph Slack message to the leadership team summarizing why the company should invest in a Claude-powered feature that drafts personalized onboarding emails for new customers. You have the following pilot data: the feature reduced onboarding email drafting time from 15 minutes to 2 minutes per customer, 68% of drafts were sent with minor edits, and customer reply rates increased by 22% compared to the old template-based emails.

Write the Slack message. Then, in a separate paragraph, explain: what did you choose to emphasize, what did you deliberately leave out, and why? Consider whether your CEO cares more about the technology or the business result.
hints: Lead with the business outcome — time saved and reply rate improvement — not the technology. Your CEO is evaluating an investment, not reviewing architecture.
The 68% acceptance rate is a positive signal, but framing it as '68% sent with minor edits' sounds better than '32% needed significant rework.' Think about how you present the same data point.
Leave out implementation details like API costs per call, model selection, or prompt engineering — those belong in a different conversation with a different audience.
Consider whether mentioning Claude by name adds value in this context. Does your CEO care about the vendor, or the outcome?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A concise Slack message (one paragraph, appropriate tone for leadership) that leads with business outcomes — time savings, productivity multiplier, and the 22% reply rate increase — rather than technology details. (2) The message should frame the pilot data compellingly without overpromising — presenting the 68% acceptance rate positively while not hiding the fact that some edits are needed. (3) A separate reflection paragraph explaining what was emphasized (business impact, scale potential), what was omitted (technical details, API costs, model specifics), and the reasoning behind those choices. Deduct points if the message reads like an engineering spec, if it leads with 'Claude' or 'AI' rather than outcomes, or if the reflection paragraph is missing or superficial.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Communicating AI value is a core PM skill that determines whether your AI features get funded, built, and adopted. Everything in this module connects back to a single principle: different audiences need fundamentally different stories about the same underlying technology.

The foundation is **knowing your audience's mental model.** Engineers think in systems and failure modes. Executives think in business outcomes and competitive positioning. Customers think in personal value and control. Your job is to translate the same capability into the language each group already thinks in.

For structuring those conversations, the **Capability-Limit-Improvement framework** gives you a reliable backbone. For every AI feature, be prepared to state what it does well, where it has boundaries, and how it gets better. This three-part structure works with every audience — only the technical depth changes.

Throughout all of these conversations, remember that **precision builds trust while vagueness destroys it.** Saying "85% accuracy in testing with 2,000 examples" is far more credible than "very accurate." Saying "generates a draft for human review" sets better expectations than "writes automatically." Qualified claims aren't weaker — they're stronger because they signal that you've actually measured what you're describing.

When you encounter resistance, **welcome the pushback.** Skeptical stakeholders sharpen your strategy. Offer time-boxed pilots, share raw data, and let skeptics choose the test cases. Evidence converts skeptics; arguments rarely do.

Finally, treat your **demos as narratives, not showcases.** Start with the pain, show the capability, acknowledge the limits, and end with the improvement trajectory. A demo that shows one realistic challenge is more credible than a demo that looks perfect — because perfection makes stakeholders wonder why you need more time to ship.
